items.question: Marie est ___. (She is Canadian.)
items.explanation: A woman is canadienne; the -ne ending marks the feminine singular.

items.question: Paul est ___. (He is Canadian.)
items.explanation: A man is canadien, the masculine singular with no -ne.

items.question: Elle est ___. (She is French.)
items.explanation: A woman's nationality is française; français is masculine and canadienne is the wrong country.

items.question: J'habite ___ Montréal.
items.explanation: Habiter takes à before a city name: j'habite à Montréal.

items.question: ___ à Montréal. (I live in Montréal.)
items.explanation: Je + habite elides to j'habite because habiter starts with a vowel sound.

items.question: Elle ___ à Montréal.
items.explanation: With elle, habiter becomes habite, with no -s.

items.question: Je parle ___. (French — the language.)
items.explanation: The language name never changes: je parle français, never française (the -aise form is only for a person).

items.question: Tu parles ___ ? (English — the language.)
items.explanation: A language name stays invariable: je parle anglais, never anglaise.

items.question: Tu ___ à Montréal ?
items.explanation: With tu, habiter adds -s: tu habites.
